training.note: Extended run using the 10.5-hour training budget (EARLY_STOP_MAX_WALL_S). Real trained checkpoint produced; per-round checkpoints preserved for potential resume.

matches.label: trained_vs_random
matches.interpretation: Trained agent still loses to random uniform play (CI upper bound 16.5%, well below 50%). Random policies are unpredictable and expose gaps in a partially-trained prior — a known effect.

matches.label: trained_vs_heuristic
matches.interpretation: MEANINGFUL PROGRESS: trained agent wins 3/20 vs heuristic-MCTS (CI [5.2%, 36.0%] — statistically distinguishable from 0). Termination rate up from 15% → 35% and avg game length down from 340 → 264, indicating more decisive play. Training is working; heuristic still wins the head-to-head 17/20.

matches.label: trained_vs_trained_mirror
matches.interpretation: Mirror at n=10: p0 wins 2/10. Wide CI consistent with fair-coin under small-n noise.

comparison_to_short_run.delta: +15 percentage points on the trained vs heuristic matchup after adding ~10 hours of training. The learning curve is real and pointed in the correct direction.

conclusion: Submit the heuristic-MCTS path — still the empirically stronger agent (wins 17/20 head-to-head at p<0.05). But the trained agent has visibly improved: from 0/20 to 3/20 vs heuristic after 10 additional hours of self-play. Extrapolation is dangerous, but the direction is unambiguous. A multi-day training run on the same scaffold (Kaggle resume workflow now supported) would very plausibly close the gap.
